title: Breadth-first and depth-first search

publication.changeSummary: Language-neutral DSA draft with explicit C/C++/Java/Python mapping for Breadth-first and depth-first search; independent algorithm, implementation, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Algorithmic Foundations; August 2023; material applicable to Breadth-first and depth-first search
sources.claim: BFS explores unweighted distance layers with a queue, while DFS explores depth with recursion or a stack.

canonicalExplanation.summary: BFS explores unweighted distance layers with a queue, while DFS explores depth with recursion or a stack.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: BFS marks a vertex when enqueued so it is not added repeatedly by multiple parents. Your goal is to implement, trace and test BFS and DFS on disconnected and cyclic graphs. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Stacks, queues and deques; Hash tables, sets and maps; Adjacency list and matrix; Stack ADT; Queue and circular buffer. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Shared concept and invariant
sections.body: BFS explores unweighted distance layers with a queue, while DFS explores depth with recursion or a stack. This claim is assessed independently from syntax so conceptual evidence can transfer.

sections.heading: C, C++, Java, and Python implementation map
sections.body: In C, represent adjacency and disjoint sets with explicit buffers and ownership; C++ uses vector-based lists and standard priority structures; Java uses typed collections and careful object equality; Python uses list/dict/set and heapq, with the same graph or partition invariant and complexity target.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Visited state timing matters, recursion can overflow, and neighbor order changes traversal sequence but not reachability. The draft therefore rejects: BFS and DFS always find the same shortest unweighted path.

includes: Maintain visited state and a queue or stack to traverse without infinite repetition.
Implement, trace and test BFS and DFS on disconnected and cyclic graphs.

In C, represent adjacency and disjoint sets with explicit buffers and ownership; C++ uses vector-based lists and standard priority structures; Java uses typed collections and careful object equality; Python uses list/dict/set and heapq, with the same graph or partition invariant and complexity target.
excludes: Visited state timing matters, recursion can overflow, and neighbor order changes traversal sequence but not reachability.

outcomes: Implement, trace and test BFS and DFS on disconnected and cyclic graphs.

examples.title: Breadth-first and depth-first search: language-neutral trace
examples.situation: BFS marks a vertex when enqueued so it is not added repeatedly by multiple parents.
walkthrough: Step 1 — State the abstract input and invariant: BFS marks a vertex when enqueued so it is not added repeatedly by multiple parents.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: BFS gives shortest edge-count paths in unweighted graphs; DFS does not generally provide that guarantee.
examples.result: The conceptual result follows because BFS gives shortest edge-count paths in unweighted graphs; DFS does not generally provide that guarantee.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: BFS and DFS always find the same shortest unweighted path.
misconceptions.correction: BFS gives shortest edge-count paths in unweighted graphs; DFS does not generally provide that guarantee.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use an invariant or counterexample to explain why this DSA belief fails: BFS and DFS always find the same shortest unweighted path.
